unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Bring together your strongest vocabulary, reading, grammar, speaking and writing from Units 1-9. Create an evidence-led inquiry product for a defined audience, attribute and evaluate sources, defend conclusions and reflect on your Grade 7 growth.
unit.learningPath: Curate and annotate evidence. Form an inquiry question. Compare and verify sources. Plan and draft cohesive sections. Revise from feedback. Present and defend. Reflect toward Grade 8. Complete the final course quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 7 learner defending a researched English capstone

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Angry and ready to fight or argue.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The belligerent dog barked loudly at anyone who came near.
practiceSentences: The belligerent dog barked loudly at anyone who came near.
In my final essay, I contrasted the belligerent villain with the patient mentor who eventually changed him.
Looking back at our debates this year, why did belligerent speakers so rarely convince anyone?
"Your character sounds belligerent in chapter one," noted Maya, "so his gentleness later surprises the reader."
I have learnt that a belligerent opening line can ruin an otherwise well-argued speech.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - l - l - i - g - e - r - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'belligerent', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Family, Identity, and History.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The wish to do evil or harm to others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The villain in the story showed malevolence by trying to hurt the hero.
practiceSentences: The villain in the story showed malevolence by trying to hurt the hero.
For the capstone anthology, Theo wrote a poem contrasting quiet malevolence with loud, harmless anger.
Which story we studied this year portrayed malevolence most convincingly, and what made it believable?
"True malevolence needs no shouting," wrote Samira in her review, "only cold, deliberate choices."
Reflecting on the novels we read, I realise malevolence is scariest when the character seems ordinary.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - l - e - v - o - l - e - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'malevolence', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Family, Identity, and History.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Wanting to hurt someone because they hurt you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After being teased, she felt vindictive and wanted to get back at her classmates.
practiceSentences: After being teased, she felt vindictive and wanted to get back at her classmates.
My final book report examined how the vindictive sea captain slowly destroyed his own crew's loyalty.
When we revised our stories this term, why did so many of us soften our vindictive characters?
"A vindictive narrator cannot be trusted," argued Adam during our last literature circle of the year.
Writing my end-of-year reflection, I decided that vindictive characters interest me more than purely evil ones.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - i - n - d - i - c - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'vindictive', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Family, Identity, and History.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make someone very angry.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It can infuriate me when people talk during a movie.
practiceSentences: It can infuriate me when people talk during a movie.
Unfinished endings infuriate some readers, yet our class anthology deliberately left two stories open.
Which revision comment would infuriate you more: careless praise or harsh but accurate criticism?
"Plot holes infuriate me," confessed Sami, "so I checked my capstone story three separate times."
I admit that losing my final draft to a computer error managed to infuriate me completely.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - f - u - r - i - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'infuriate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Family, Identity, and History.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Kind and generous; wanting to help others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.
practiceSentences: The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.
The benevolent librarian who guided our research all year deserved the dedication page of our anthology.
Should a story's benevolent character ever have flaws, or does perfection make them boring?
"Our benevolent sponsor donated the printing costs," announced the headteacher at the celebration assembly.
Looking back, the most benevolent act I witnessed this year was quiet and completely unrewarded.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - n - e - v - o - l - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'benevolent', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Celebrations and Festivals.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The charismatic leader inspired everyone to work together for a better school.
practiceSentences: The charismatic leader inspired everyone to work together for a better school.
The charismatic host of our poetry evening kept the audience engaged between every single performance.
Does a charismatic speaker always deserve our trust, or should we still examine their evidence carefully?
"Being charismatic is not enough," our teacher reminded us, "a presentation still needs solid content."
After a year of class speeches, I believe charismatic delivery can be learnt through honest practice.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - h - a - r - i - s - m - a - t - i - c
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'charismatic', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Celebrations and Festivals.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She was determined to finish her homework before going out to play.
practiceSentences: She was determined to finish her homework before going out to play.
Samira stayed determined through five drafts until her capstone story finally sounded like her own voice.
What kept you determined during the hardest assignment of the year, and would it work again?
"Stay determined through the slow middle chapters," advised Theo, "because endings reward patient writers."
I feel more determined now than in September, because I have watched my own writing improve.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - t - e - r - m - i - n - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'determined', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Celebrations and Festivals.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Working hard and being careful to do things well.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She is a diligent student who always completes her homework on time.
practiceSentences: She is a diligent student who always completes her homework on time.
The diligent editors of our class anthology checked every comma before the final printing deadline.
How does a diligent reviser decide which favourite sentences must be cut from a final draft?
"Your diligent note-taking saved our group project," Maya told Sami after the presentations ended.
Honestly, being diligent with small daily tasks helped me more than any last-minute effort.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - l - i - g - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'diligent', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Celebrations and Festivals.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: "Isaac Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers" is a famous example of alliteration.
practiceSentences: "Isaac Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers" is a famous example of alliteration.
For the capstone reading, Adam chose a poem whose alliteration echoed the hiss of desert wind.
Where does alliteration stop being musical and start distracting the reader from the meaning?
"Notice the alliteration in my title," said Samira proudly, "it took me an entire evening."
Reviewing my portfolio, I see I rely on alliteration whenever I want a line to feel playful.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - l - l - i - t - e - r - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'alliteration', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A word that has the same ending sound as another word.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In the poem, "cat" and "hat" are examples of words that rhyme.
practiceSentences: In the poem, "cat" and "hat" are examples of words that rhyme.
Our final anthology includes one poem where the rhyme appears only in the first and last stanzas.
Does a poem need rhyme to be memorable, or can strong rhythm alone carry it?
"Force a rhyme and the reader hears the effort," warned our teacher during revision week.
After experimenting all year, I prefer half-rhyme because a perfect rhyme can sound too neat.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - h - y - m - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rhyme', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The drummer kept a steady rhythm that made everyone want to dance.
practiceSentences: The drummer kept a steady rhythm that made everyone want to dance.
Reading her capstone poem aloud, Maya adjusted the rhythm until every line matched her breathing.
How does changing a single syllable alter the rhythm of an otherwise finished line?
"Clap the rhythm before you write anything down," suggested Sami during our final poetry workshop.
I discovered this year that rhythm matters in speeches just as much as in poems.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - h - y - t - h - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rhythm', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Doing or saying something again and again.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In class, we practise our vocabulary through repetition to remember the words better.
practiceSentences: In class, we practise our vocabulary through repetition to remember the words better.
The winning speech used repetition of one short phrase to build feeling towards its final line.
When does repetition strengthen a poem, and when does it simply reveal a limited imagination?
"Deliberate repetition is a drumbeat," our teacher said, "while accidental repetition is a stumble."
Looking through my portfolio, I notice repetition became my favourite persuasive technique this year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - p - e - t - i - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'repetition', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A feeling of sadness or being unhappy for a long time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After her friend moved away, she felt a deep sense of melancholy.
practiceSentences: After her friend moved away, she felt a deep sense of melancholy.
A gentle melancholy runs through the final section of our anthology, where we wrote about farewells.
Why does melancholy music sometimes comfort us more effectively than cheerful songs do?
"There is melancholy in your closing paragraph," Samira observed, "but also a quiet kind of hope."
Finishing my last journal entry, I felt an unexpected melancholy about leaving Grade 7 behind.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - l - a - n - c - h - o - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'melancholy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Full of energy, excitement, and happiness.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The children were exuberant as they played in the park on a sunny day.
practiceSentences: The children were exuberant as they played in the park on a sunny day.
The exuberant applause after the final performance told us the anthology launch had truly succeeded.
Can an exuberant narrator describe a sad event convincingly, or does the tone clash?
"Your exuberant opening paragraph made me smile before breakfast," wrote the teacher in her feedback.
Reading my September entries, I sound so exuberant that I barely recognise my own writing.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - u - b - e - r - a - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'exuberant', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very happy and excited about something good that has happened.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She was elated when she found out she had won the competition.
practiceSentences: She was elated when she found out she had won the competition.
Theo was elated when the printed anthology finally arrived, still smelling faintly of fresh ink.
Were you more elated by finishing the capstone or by hearing the audience's warm response?
"I am completely elated," laughed Maya, holding the certificate above her head after the ceremony.
Honestly, I felt elated for days after my poem was chosen to open the collection.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - l - a - t - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'elated', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Able to be easily hurt or harmed, either physically or emotionally.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The little bird was vulnerable because it had just fallen from its nest.
practiceSentences: The little bird was vulnerable because it had just fallen from its nest.
Sharing a personal memoir makes any writer vulnerable, yet honesty is what moved the audience most.
Why do the most vulnerable moments in a story often become the ones readers remember?
"Feeling vulnerable on stage is normal," the drama coach assured us before the capstone reading.
I learnt that admitting confusion made me vulnerable but also helped my whole group improve.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - u - l - n - e - r - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'vulnerable', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To accept or admit that something is true, or to recognise that you have received something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I want to acknowledge that you did a great job on your project.
practiceSentences: I want to acknowledge that you did a great job on your project.
In her closing speech, Samira paused to acknowledge every helper, from editors to the caretaker.
Why is it sometimes so difficult to acknowledge a mistake in front of the whole class?
"We must acknowledge our sources properly," the librarian reminded us during the final research check.
I now acknowledge that the feedback I once resented actually shaped my best work this year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - c - k - n - o - w - l - e - d - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'acknowledge', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To work together with others to achieve a common goal.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The students will collaborate to create posters about protecting the environment.
practiceSentences: The students will collaborate to create posters about protecting the environment.
Five student teams learned to collaborate across year groups to produce the capstone exhibition.
Is it harder to collaborate with a close friend or with someone you barely know?
"When we collaborate honestly," said Adam, "the final story belongs to all of us equally."
Looking back, learning to collaborate taught me more patience than any individual assignment did.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - l - l - a - b - o - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'collaborate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To show how something works or to prove something clearly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The ranger will demonstrate how to set up a tent safely.
practiceSentences: The ranger will demonstrate how to set up a tent safely.
The capstone portfolio asks each student to demonstrate growth across reading, writing, and speaking.
How will you demonstrate that your persuasive skills have improved since the autumn term?
"Let me demonstrate the editing shortcut," offered Sami, turning his screen towards the group.
I chose my two contrasting essays because together they demonstrate how far my voice has travelled.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - m - o - n - s - t - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'demonstrate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A serious effort or attempt to do something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Learning to navigate with a compass was a worthwhile endeavour.
practiceSentences: Learning to navigate with a compass was a worthwhile endeavour.
Producing a hundred-page anthology was the most ambitious endeavour our year group has ever attempted.
Which endeavour taught you more this year: the group documentary or the individual memoir?
"Every worthwhile endeavour begins badly," our teacher joked, holding up her own messy first draft.
I regard learning public speaking as an endeavour worth every uncomfortable moment it cost me.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - d - e - a - v - o - u - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'endeavour', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A way of explaining something by comparing it to something else that is similar.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher used an analogy to explain how a computer works by comparing it to a brain.
practiceSentences: The teacher used an analogy to explain how a computer works by comparing it to a brain.
In his farewell speech, the headteacher used an analogy comparing our year group to a river gathering strength.
Which analogy best captures revision: sculpting stone, pruning a garden, or tuning an instrument?
"Your analogy between drafting and mapmaking really clarified the process," Maya told her partner.
I find a good analogy can rescue an explanation that facts alone leave lifeless.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - a - l - o - g - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'analogy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Sports, Olympics, and Achievement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Someone who likes to argue or disagree with others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My friend can be very argumentative when we talk about our favourite movies.
practiceSentences: My friend can be very argumentative when we talk about our favourite movies.
The argumentative essay was the hardest capstone piece, because every claim demanded solid evidence.
Is being argumentative a flaw in friendship but a strength in formal debate?
"You are argumentative today," laughed Samira, "so you should definitely join the debating society."
I confess I was argumentative during peer review until I realised my partner was usually right.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - g - u - m - e - n - t - a - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'argumentative', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Sports, Olympics, and Achievement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher tried to be fair, but some students felt she had a bias towards the football players.
practiceSentences: The teacher tried to be fair, but some students felt she had a bias towards the football players.
Our media project revealed how easily bias slips into headlines through a single loaded phrase.
How can a careful reader detect bias in an article that sounds perfectly reasonable?
"Check your own bias first," advised the librarian, "before you judge the author's motives."
Reviewing my persuasive essay, I found a bias I had never noticed while writing it.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - i - a - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'bias', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Sports, Olympics, and Achievement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Facts or information that help prove something is true.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The detective found evidence that showed who took the missing money.
practiceSentences: The detective found evidence that showed who took the missing money.
The strongest capstone essays supported every major claim with evidence drawn from at least two sources.
What counts as reliable evidence when two respected websites completely contradict each other?
"Show me the evidence," our teacher would say, "and then I will consider your conclusion."
I now collect evidence before forming opinions, a habit this year's research project gave me.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - v - i - d - e - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'evidence', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Sports, Olympics, and Achievement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A strong desire to achieve something important in life.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Her ambition is to become a doctor who helps children in rural areas.
practiceSentences: Her ambition is to become a doctor who helps children in rural areas.
Maya's ambition to publish a novel began with the short story she wrote for the anthology.
Does ambition without discipline achieve anything, or does it simply remain a pleasant daydream?
"My ambition is to study engineering and design flood defences for coastal towns," announced Theo.
Writing my reflection, I realised my ambition has grown quieter but far more focused this year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - m - b - i - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'ambition', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Success, Careers, and Personal Growth.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Being fully committed to a task or purpose; giving time and energy to something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: His dedication to studying every evening helped him pass all his exams.
practiceSentences: His dedication to studying every evening helped him pass all his exams.
The anthology's final page thanks the teachers whose dedication kept the project alive through setbacks.
How do you maintain dedication to a long project once the early excitement fades?
"Talent starts the race," said the coach, "but dedication is what finishes it."
I believe my dedication to reading every evening improved my writing more than any single lesson.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - d - i - c - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'dedication', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Success, Careers, and Personal Growth.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Through perseverance, she learned to speak English fluently.
practiceSentences: Through perseverance, she learned to speak English fluently.
Sami's perseverance through seven drafts turned a rejected story into the anthology's closing piece.
Where does perseverance come from when a task feels endless and nobody is watching?
"Perseverance is just patience with a purpose," Grandmother told me when I nearly quit.
Looking back, I value the perseverance I built this year more than the grades themselves.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - r - s - e - v - e - r - a - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'perseverance', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Success, Careers, and Personal Growth.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Winning the science prize was her greatest achievement this year.
practiceSentences: Winning the science prize was her greatest achievement this year.
Completing the capstone anthology felt like a shared achievement rather than a collection of individual marks.
Which achievement this year cost you the most effort yet brought the least public praise?
"This certificate honours a genuine achievement," said the headteacher, shaking each student's hand.
For me, speaking confidently in assembly was a bigger achievement than any written grade.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - c - h - i - e - v - e - m - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'achievement', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Success, Careers, and Personal Growth.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To show or tell what you think or feel.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I want to express my ideas in the school magazine.
practiceSentences: I want to express my ideas in the school magazine.
The capstone taught us to express complicated feelings precisely instead of hiding behind vague phrases.
How do you express disagreement respectfully when the whole group already seems decided?
"Poetry lets me express what ordinary conversation cannot," wrote Adam in his final reflection.
I find it easier to express gratitude in writing than to say it aloud.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - p - r - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'express', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Media, Technology, and News.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say that something is true, or to ask for something you believe is yours.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She will claim her prize after winning the contest.
practiceSentences: She will claim her prize after winning the contest.
Every persuasive paragraph in the portfolio must open with a clear claim the writer can defend.
If an advertisement makes a bold claim, what evidence should we demand before believing it?
"Support that claim or remove it," the peer editor wrote in the margin of my essay.
I have learnt to test each claim I read online against at least one independent source.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - a - i - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'claim', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Media, Technology, and News.

readings.title: Review text 1: Family, Identity, and History: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Year 7 English at Ehel Academy! This is Unit 1: Family, Identity, and History. In this unit, you will explore some of the most important questions a person can ask: Where do I come from? Who are the people in my family? What stories have been passed down through the generations? You will read about families, learn how to describe your own background, and discover how people across East Africa and the wider world have moved, settled, and built new lives over the centuries.
Family is at the heart of every culture. In Somali culture, for example, knowing your ancestry is deeply important. Many people can name their great-great-grandparents and trace their family line back many generations. In other East African communities, family histories are kept alive through oral stories, songs, and special objects passed from one generation to the next. This unit will help you explore these ideas in English, building your reading, writing, grammar, and speaking skills along the way.
You will also study the past simple tense, which is essential for telling stories about what happened in the past. Whether you are describing your grandmother's journey across a desert or explaining how your family settled in a new city, the past simple tense is your most important tool. Alongside the past simple, you will learn about present perfect vs past simple, apostrophes for possession, and defining relative clauses to add detail to your sentences.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a passage about family history and migration, and discuss its ideas in English.
Use the past simple tense correctly to describe completed actions and events.
Distinguish between the past simple and the present perfect tense.
Use apostrophes correctly to show possession (e.g., my grandmother's house, the families' traditions).
Use defining relative clauses to give essential information about people and things.
Write a detailed family biography using precise vocabulary and correct grammar.
Present your family history clearly in a short spoken presentation.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the lessons in the Grammar file (Past Simple, Present Perfect vs Past Simple, Apostrophes, Defining Relative Clauses, Should / Shouldn't).
Read the Story file, "The Fire Behind the Fence", and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all nine sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it. Remember to say each word out loud as you learn it. Hearing yourself speak the word helps your brain remember it.
Reading: The Journey of Nadia's Family
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words and notice how the past simple tense is used throughout. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions and explore the grammar of the passage.
Nadia sat on the floor of her grandmother's sitting room in Hargeisa, her legs crossed beneath her. The evening air carried the smell of bariis iyo hilib from the kitchen. Ayeeyo settled into her favourite chair and smiled.

readings.title: Review text 2: The Hero of Kitale, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was early on a Monday morning in April when Samira rose before the sun. The room was still dark, and the only sound was the soft breathing of her younger brother, Sami, asleep on a straw mat beside her bed. Their mud-brick house sat near the edge of Kitale, close to the maize farms that spread out like a green sea across the land.
Samira, now fourteen, had become known in their school as both diligent and punctual. Every teacher said the same thing: “Samira is a paragon of responsibility.” But Samira didn’t care much about praise. What she cared about was the community.
Their town had faced many problems: broken water taps, rising food prices, and a school library whose roof had fallen in during the last rainy season. Some students complained, but Samira chose to act.
Her teacher, Mr. Sami, was a charismatic man who believed in youth leadership. One day, he announced that the school would be holding its first ever Student Leadership Election. Posters appeared around the compound with names of candidates and bright slogans.
Some candidates were loud and zealous, others charming, and one boy named Adam was incredibly charismatic. Adam came from a prominent family. His uncle owned the largest hardware store in Kitale. His campaign posters were printed, not handwritten. He offered sweets to younger students and even brought a speaker to play music at lunchtime.
“Wow,” whispered Sami as the music played. “Adam is legendary.”
Samira smiled. “He may be loud, but we need a leader who is authentic.”
Samira did not have money for speakers or printed posters. What she had was a notebook, a group of close friends, and ideas. She spent her evenings creating meticulous, hand-drawn posters. They showed a tree growing from dry ground with the words: “Real leaders endure. They don’t just promise. They act.”
Her speeches were not flashy. But they were honest. “We must fix the roof,” she told the school. “We need more books. I cannot promise you music, but I promise to work.”
Samira’s words began to spread. Students whispered about how determined she seemed. Even some teachers mentioned her name in staff meetings. “That girl,” said Ms. Sophia, “has the spirit of a lion. She’s so intrepid.”
On the day of the election, the school hall was filled with a clamor of voices. Adam walked in first, wearing a brightly-coloured, fur-lined jacket. Samira walked in later, wearing her usual neat school uniform, her braids tied back, and a quiet smile on her face.
When the votes were counted, it was close. But Samira won.
Some students cheered. Others were surprised. Adam looked disappointed but shook her hand.
From that day on, Samira became a different kind of leader. She stayed late at school on Thursdays to help younger students with reading. She worked with the head teacher to organise a book drive. Her friend Adam, who was industrious, helped carry donated books every Friday.
Samira also helped reconcile students who had quarrelled. Once, two girls fought over a missing pen. Samira sat with them under the tree near the tap and helped them talk it out. “We must be impartial,” she reminded them. “We judge by facts, not emotions.”
That same week, she helped build a small garden behind the kitchen, teaching others how to grow sukuma wiki. She asked Mr. Sami to support them, and he agreed. Her leadership was quiet but steady.

readings.title: Review text 3: A Voice in the Valley, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Lomi was nervous. But she agreed.
She prepared her lesson carefully. She used coloured chalk to write examples on the blackboard. She explained that metaphors, like “The moon is a mirror,” make writing richer. She asked, “What does ‘The wind danced’ mean?”
A hand shot up. “It’s personification!”
During her lesson, she used examples from her story. “Good writers emphasize emotion. They use dialogue to make readers feel connected. A well-placed simile, like ‘as brave as a lion’, helps readers imagine better.”
She also showed students how to infer meaning from a sentence and how to structure their stories in chronological order.
Later that day, Isaac teased her. “Now you’re the teacher!”
“Only a little,” she laughed. “But it feels good.”
News of her teaching spread. Lomi was invited to speak at a youth arts event in the town nearby. She almost said no.
“I can’t. What if I fail?”
“You might fail,” Isaac said. “But your story might be remembered, too. That’s a risk worth taking.”
At the event, she read a new poem filled with rhythm, rhyme, and heart. Her words flowed like river water. The crowd was silent, listening.
One judge stood and made an assertion: “This poem must be shared with schools across the region.”
The next month, it was. Lomi’s poem was printed in three school newsletters.
When reporters visited her school, Lomi was asked, “How did you start?”
She said, “With a notebook I hid under my bed.”
Lomi’s success inspired others. Her classmate Haile, who had never shared a poem before, wrote a piece full of figurative beauty. Sophia began a comic strip series. Even shy Bilen submitted an essay about her grandfather’s garden.
Miss Nadia told the class, “Creativity should be encouraged, not hidden. And sometimes, a story must be told so that others can find the courage to tell theirs.”
The writing club, which had once been empty, now had over twenty students.
At the end of the term, a ceremony was held at the community hall. Lomi was called to the stage to receive a certificate of excellence. A banner behind her read: “The Pen Paints What the Voice Feels.”
As she held the certificate, she thought of all the students who once feared to speak.
“This,” she said softly, “is not just for me. It’s for every quiet voice that dares to speak.”
In the weeks that followed, the journal grew. More pages, more stories, more young writers who felt brave enough to write. Lomi began helping to edit the submissions, teaching others about punctuation and tone. “Even punctuation,” she told one younger student, “can shape how a story is perceived.”
She also began writing a new story titled “Voices in the Dust,” a tale that characterised a group of children from different regions who overcame silence with art. Her new story included strong exposition, expressive dialogue, and a clear thesis: that art can heal what fear breaks.
Lomi reflected on how language could change minds. Words could persuade gently or impose unfairly, depending on how they were used. And in her stories, she chose to persuade — to offer kindness, strength, and truth.
By the following year, Selota School had become known as the “Valley of Voices,” a place where young writers were born. One of Lomi’s favourite moments came when a younger girl handed her a poem titled “My Grandmother’s Hands,” filled with repetition and loving imagery.

readings.title: Review text 4: A Heart in the Noise, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In the dusty lanes of Mwananyamala, on the edge of Dar es Salaam, the air was always full — of smoke from chapati stalls, music from boda bodas, and voices rising in greeting or complaint. Life moved quickly. Sometimes too quickly.
Thirteen-year-old Hannah stood in front of the community kiosk where her mother sold phone credit and chewing gum. Her shoulders slumped as she stared at her crumpled math test. A red "37%" was circled at the top.
Her teacher's comment — "You must improve your discipline" — rang in her ears.
Around her, the world buzzed with the exuberant energy of boys playing football. It made her sadness feel heavier. Their laughter sounded like a cruel echo.
She stuffed the paper into her backpack.
Unfortunately, she had no words for how embarrassed she felt.
At home, her father, Baba Sami, was cleaning his tools. He repaired shoes in a small open workshop. He looked up as she walked by.
He noticed her voice and frowned but said nothing.
The truth was: Hannah had stopped studying weeks ago. She didn't know why. Maybe it was the noise at home. Maybe it was the temptation to scroll through her phone instead of revising. Maybe it was the feeling that nothing really mattered.
She had become complacent without even noticing.
That evening, she sat on the roof with her younger brother. They watched the sunset, which painted the buildings with ephemeral gold.
"Do you think I'm smart?" she asked him.
He blinked. "I don't know. But you're kind."
Kind. The word made her eyes sting. It felt like a small remnant of hope.
Later, at dinner, her parents talked about the rising cost of flour. Her mother looked tired. Her father looked older.
Sophia stared at her plate. She felt vulnerable, like a balloon drifting away without string.
That night, she dreamed she was in a huge hall where people were shouting, dancing, singing — a crescendo of chaos — and she was the only one trying to read a book.
She woke up feeling melancholy, her heart heavy like soaked cloth.
Over the next week, her emotions fluctuated like the tides. She was happy one morning, then anxious by lunchtime. Sometimes she was elated when a teacher praised her, but the feeling never lasted. She envied friends who seemed stable, focused.
In English class, the teacher asked students to describe a recent emotional moment using a story. Hannah didn't raise her hand.
But later that day, she began to write.
She described her dream. She described the hall and the noise. She added that the book in her hands had no words, only blank pages.
Her teacher, Ms. Amal — who had once worked as a journalist — read the story and clapped.
"This must be published in the school paper," she said. "It captures confusion with such candor."
Sophia felt heat rush to her face. But this time, it wasn't shame.
That afternoon, as she walked home, she passed a group of older students shouting jokes. One boy was being obnoxious, calling names. Another girl tried to walk away, but he blocked her.
She stepped forward. "Leave her alone."
The boy laughed. "You? Little Hannah?"
But something in her voice had changed. It wasn't loud — but it was certain.

readings.title: Review text 5: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The journey took three hours by bus. When the students finally stepped onto the forest trail, the air felt different — cooler, damper, and full of unfamiliar sounds. Tall trees stretched upward, their branches forming a thick canopy that blocked most of the sunlight. Birds called from hidden perches, and somewhere in the distance, a stream gurgled over rocks.
Samira, one of the keenest hikers, was the first to notice the damage. Along the edge of the trail, several large trees had been cut down. Their stumps were pale and raw, surrounded by scattered branches. “Deforestation,” she whispered to her friend Adam. “Someone has been logging here illegally.”
Ustadh Isaac gathered the group. “This is exactly what I wanted you to see,” he said seriously. “The Boni Forest is home to rare animals and hundreds of plant species. When trees are removed, the entire ecosystem suffers. Birds lose their nests. The soil washes away in the rain. Rivers become polluted with mud. Biodiversity decreases.”
He asked the students to take notes and photographs to document what they observed. They would use this evidence later to create environmental awareness materials for their school and community.
That afternoon, a local ranger named Adam joined the group. He explained how conservation efforts were helping the forest recover. “We have established a tree-planting programme,” he said. “Every month, volunteers plant two hundred seedlings. Protecting the forest is not one person’s job — it requires collaboration.”
The students set up their campsite near a clearing. They collected firewood that had already fallen — they did not break any living branches. They filtered water from the stream and cooked a simple meal of rice, beans and chapati. As the sun set, the sky turned from gold to deep violet, and the first stars appeared. Adam pointed upward. “SubhanAllah,” he said quietly. “You cannot see stars like this in the city.”
Around the campfire, Ustadh Isaac asked each student to share one thing they had learned. “I learned that protecting nature is not just about big organisations,” said Samira. “It starts with us — with small choices, like not littering, like planting a tree, like speaking up when something is wrong.”
The next morning, the group joined the ranger’s team to plant thirty saplings along the damaged section of the trail. Each student dug a hole, placed a seedling carefully inside, and covered the roots with soil. It was hard, rewarding work.
On the bus home, the students were tired but changed. They had seen the beauty of the natural world and the threats it faces. They understood that sustainability is not just a word in a textbook — it is a responsibility. And they were ready to become the guardians their teacher had asked them to be.
Answer these questions in full sentences:
According to Ustadh Isaac, what happens to an ecosystem when trees are removed? Give at least three effects.
In your own words, explain what the title "Guardians of the Green" means.
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what it means in your own words:
These questions ask you to go beyond the text and share your own ideas:
The -ing form of a verb is one of the most versatile tools in English. In this section you will learn three important ways to use -ing forms. Detailed lessons with more examples and practice are in your separate Grammar file.

grammar.title: Sentence accuracy audit
grammar.explanation: Review agreement, tense and complete sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Every sentence needs a clear subject and verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Correct six sentences from your portfolio.

grammar.title: Tense control
grammar.explanation: Check that past, present and future time are clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Yesterday I visited. Today I learn. Tomorrow I will present.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Revise six verbs and explain each tense choice.

grammar.title: Questions and responses
grammar.explanation: Use accurate Wh- and yes/no questions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Where did you learn this? Why is it important?
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Write and answer six audience questions.

grammar.title: Description and precision
grammar.explanation: Improve nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: The thoughtful learner explained the idea clearly.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Joining ideas
grammar.explanation: Connect ideas with and, but, because, so and when.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I revised my story because feedback helped me.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Final editing conference
grammar.explanation: Apply capitals, spelling, punctuation and paragraphing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read, mark, revise, then read again.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

writing.title: Portfolio evidence annotations
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a precise paragraph about each of four selected pieces and explain what each demonstrates.
writing.modelText: This revised report shows that I can organize evidence, evaluate a viewpoint and explain the effect of my choices.
writing.expectedLength: Four to six cohesive paragraphs with source evidence
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Inquiry proposal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your question, audience, approved sources, product and plan in four organized paragraphs.
writing.modelText: My inquiry will investigate how communities can use digital tools responsibly by comparing two approved sources.
writing.expectedLength: Four to six cohesive paragraphs with source evidence
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Evidence-led first draft

writing.expectedLength: Four to six cohesive paragraphs with source evidence
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Substantive revision from feedback
writing.promptAndInstructions: Record four feedback points and rewrite the sections they improve.
writing.modelText: Feedback asked me to compare source reliability, so I distinguished evidence from opinion and explained why one source was stronger.
writing.sentenceStarter: One substantial improvement I made was...
writing.expectedLength: Four to six cohesive paragraphs with source evidence
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Presentation and defence notes

writing.modelText: Opening; inquiry question; claims; attributed evidence; viewpoint; counterargument; audience questions; closing.

writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized and attributed relevant evidence; evaluated source reliability and viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use an inquiry organizer, source record, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Grade 7 reflection

writing.extension: Evaluate competing source choices, address a counterargument, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

quizzes.question: What does 'belligerent' mean?
quizzes.options: A strong feeling of dislike or hatred towards someone. | Angry and ready to fight or argue. | To make someone very angry. | Wanting to hurt someone because they hurt you.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Angry and ready to fight or argue.
quizzes.explanation: The belligerent dog barked loudly at anyone who came near.

quizzes.question: What does 'malevolence' mean?
quizzes.options: A strong feeling of dislike or hatred towards someone. | Someone who is very interested in themselves and thinks they are better than others. | The wish to do evil or harm to others. | To make someone very angry.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The wish to do evil or harm to others.
quizzes.explanation: The villain in the story showed malevolence by trying to hurt the hero.

quizzes.question: What does 'benevolent' mean?
quizzes.options: Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up. | Kind and generous; wanting to help others. | Showing great excitement and interest about something. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Kind and generous; wanting to help others.
quizzes.explanation: The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.

quizzes.question: What does 'charismatic' mean?
quizzes.options: Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. | Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people. | Showing great excitement and interest about something. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people.
quizzes.explanation: The charismatic leader inspired everyone to work together for a better school.

quizzes.question: What does 'alliteration' mean?
quizzes.options: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement. | Doing or saying something again and again. | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
quizzes.explanation: "Isaac Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers" is a famous example of alliteration.

quizzes.question: What does 'rhyme' mean?
quizzes.options: A word that has the same ending sound as another word. | Doing or saying something again and again. | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A word that has the same ending sound as another word.
quizzes.explanation: In the poem, "cat" and "hat" are examples of words that rhyme.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Review text 2: The Hero of Kitale, part 1': Sami, was a _____ man who believed in youth leadership.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Sami, was a charismatic man who believed in youth leadership.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Review text 3: A Voice in the Valley, part 2': That’s a risk worth taking.” At the event, she read a new poem filled with rhythm, _____, and heart.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: That’s a risk worth taking.” At the event, she read a new poem filled with rhythm, rhyme, and heart.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Review text 4: A Heart in the Noise, part 1': She woke up feeling _____, her heart heavy like soaked cloth.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: She woke up feeling melancholy, her heart heavy like soaked cloth.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.explanation: The text says: Around her, the world buzzed with the exuberant energy of boys playing football.
